turns.text: Do you remember it at all? Do you remember that he was accused?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I can no longer remember whether he was accused. It may be.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Do you know where he died?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: No, I do not know.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Do you know that he was interned in Buchenwald after the Reichstag fire and remained there until he died in 1944? Did you know that?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes, I remember it was said he was a victim of an air attack.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And where was he when he was caught in this air attack?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Where was Thalmann? I did not quite understand the question.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Where was he when he became a victim of an air attack?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: As far as I heard, he was said to be in the Buchenwald concentration camp.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And how long had he been there?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: That I do not know; I have no knowledge of that.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Were you present at the conversation between Thalmann and Goering?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What did he complain about then in the concentration camp?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: About treatment during interrogations.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: That was the only complaint he made?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes, as far as I can remember. The Reich Marshal asked him whether he had good food and whether he was properly treated. All these things were discussed.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And Thalmann found no fault with the concentration camp except treatment during interrogation?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes; as far as I remember that was his chief complaint.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Were the Communists regarded by the Nazis as enemies of the country?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And concentration camps, then, were built to receive Communists among others, were they not?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And Jews?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes, as far as they were known to be enemies of the State.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Were Jews also regarded as enemies of the State?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Generally not; only when they had been recognized as such.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Recognized as such -- what, as Jews?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: No, if a Jew was recognized as an enemy of the State, he was treated as an enemy of the State.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What was the test as to whether he was an enemy of the State?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Well, his attitude, his active participation in actions hostile to the State.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Such as what? What actions?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I cannot give any details. I was not Chief of the Gestapo, and therefore I do not know any details.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Were you not with Goering as his secretary during the time he was Chief of the Gestapo?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: In April 1933 I became State Secretary in the Prussian State Ministry.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And did you not have to do with concentration camps under the secret police as such?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: No, I had nothing to do with that.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Who handled that for Goering?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: The then Ministerialdirektor Diels.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did you know that, in setting up the Secret State Police, Goering used SS men to man the Gestapo?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I cannot remember that any more.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You were a member of the SS, were you not?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What was your office in the SS?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I never held any office in the SS, neither was I in charge of an SS formation. I was just a member of the SS.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Were you not ObergruppenFuehrer?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes, I was an SS-ObergruppenFuehrer.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, as to these unauthorized concentration camps, you were asked who set them up, and I do not think you answered. Will you tell us about who set up these concentration camps?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I remember two camps. In the case of one, I know for certain it was GruppenFuehrer Heines, in Breslau.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: GruppenFuehrer of what?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: SA-GruppenFuehrer Heines, in Breslau.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Who was the other?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I cannot say exactly. I believe it was Karpfenstein, but I am not sure of it.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Who was he?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Karpfenstein was Gauleiter in Stettin.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And the Gauleiter was a Party official?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes, he was a Party official.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And the concentration camps were designed to take care of not only enemies of the State but enemies of the Party, were they not?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: The Prime Minister of Prussia was the Chief of the Secret State Police?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And in his absence the State Secretary of the State Ministry was to act as Chief of the Secret State Police?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: No, that was Diels.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Was that not the law, whatever was done about it? Did you not know that that was the law under which the Secret State Police was set up, Section 1, Paragraph 2?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I cannot remember that law any more. I no longer know the details.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Do you know the law of 30 November 1933? You do not know the law under which you were operating?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I do not remember that law now. I would have to see it again.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, what was wrong with these concentration camps that they had to be closed down?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: These unauthorized concentration camps had been established without permission of the then Prussian Prime Minister and for that reason he prohibited them immediately.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: That is the only reason, that they were set up without this authority?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I believe so; yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And he had them stopped immediately?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Stopped; yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Goering did not tolerate concentration camps that were not under his control and the Fuehrer backed him up in it, is that right?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, from time to time complaints came to you about the treatment of people in concentration camps, during all the time you were with Goering, did they not?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes, there were frequent complaints.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What did they complain of?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Various things.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Tell the Tribunal what the complaints were with which you had to deal.

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Well, mostly from relatives of the people taken to concentration camps whose release was applied for; or complaints that these people had been taken to a concentration camp without reason.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: That is, that they were innocent people, innocent of any offense?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: The relatives asserted this.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did you do anything to get them released from concentration camps?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: The Reich Marshal had ordered that all complaints were to be replied to. Every case was followed up at once.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And did you find that many of these people were innocent, or did you find that they were guilty?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: If anybody was found to have been wrongly taken to a concentration camp he was released immediately.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And to whom was the communication given, that he had been found innocent and was to be released from the concentration camp?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: It was given to the Secret State Police. 1VIR. JUSTICE JACKSON: To whom at the Secret State Police? Who was the man you communicated with?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I cannot name the individual who dealt with these matters. The chief, as far as I remember, was first Heydrich and then Kaltenbrunner or Miller.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Goering was on good terms with all of those, was he not?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well acquainted with all of those men?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Of course.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, when you say that Goering obtained the release of people from concentration camps, are you talking about just one or two cases or did he obtain the release of a good many people?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: In the course of the years, there were naturally several cases.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What do you mean by "several"?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Well, I cannot give the number now, but there were quite a lot of releases.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did you find any where the people were guilty when you investigated?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: If they could not be released, then they were guilty somehow.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Who decided that?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: That, as far as I know, was decided by the chief men of the Secret State Police.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well, then, what did you do in requesting their release? Did you advise the Secret State Police that you disagreed with their conclusion that the man was guilty, or did Goering simply order the man to be released or request his release?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: No, they were told the exact reason why the man should be released.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Do you know of any instance in which Goering requested the release of a person from a concentration camp, where it was not granted?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I cannot say that now. I have to think it over.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You cannot recall any today, can you, in which Goering's word requesting a release was not honored?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: At the moment I cannot remember any particular case.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: How many people were put in concentration camps as a result of the Rohm revolt?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: That I cannot say either.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: How many people were killed as a result of it?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I cannot say from memory. As far as I know, the figures were published at the time.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE. JACKSON
turns.text: Well, would it be a couple of hundred people that were killed for it?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I should not like to tie myself to a figure, because I may be wrong.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well, it was a very large number of people was it not?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: No, I am sure it was not a very large number.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Give a figure.

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: The number was published at the time. This could still be checked.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well, why did the Reich Marshal want Hitler to stop punishing the people who had been a party to the Rohm revolt?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I did not quite understand the question.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I understood your testimony to be that the Reich Marshal went to Hitler at some time and wanted this campaign against people who were in the Rohm revolt to be stopped. And I want to know why he wanted it stopped?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: In order to prevent innocent people being involved. Only the really guilty were to be caught and punished accordingly. It was clear that someone or other might seize this opportunity to take personal revenge and do away with his enemy, and in order to prevent this the action should be stopped immediately and only ordinary courts should deal with the matter.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Who was in charge of the selection of the people who were shot or otherwise killed as a result of the Rohm revolt?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: The Fuehrer himself.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And the Reich Marshal had sufficient influence to stop that immediately when he complained?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: At that time, yes, the Reich Marshal definitely had sufficient influence.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: In connection with this Four Year Plan you said that it was its function to regulate the confusion in the labor market?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, you represented the Reich Marshal at many meetings, did you not?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And was not one of your functions to get prisoners of war to work in the armament industry and other industries that needed labor?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You never had anything to do with that?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: No. The Plenipotentiary General for the Allocation of Labor of course applied for prisoners of war for labor.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You attended many meetings when that was discussed, did you not?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I cannot recall that.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did you report to the Reich Marshal what happened at those meetings?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: When questions of a general nature were discussed, a report was made and submitted to the Reich Marshal.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You were a member of the Central Planning Board, were you not?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And you were representing the Reich Marshal on that Board?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: No. I did not represent the Reich Marshal there, It was a board of three men -- Minister Speer , Field Marshal Milch and myself. The Central Planning Board was set up in the spring of 1942.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Who appointed you?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: The three of us were appointed to the Central Planning Board.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Who appointed you?

turns.speaker: KORNBR
turns.text: As far as I remember, Goering.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And you reported to him, did you not, what occurred from time to time?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: The Central Planning Board was merely an office for the distribution of raw materials. We usually met every 3 months in order to fix the quotas for the following quarter. Previously the office of the Four Year Plan, in co-operation with the Minister of Economics, handled the distribution and, from the spring of 1942 on, the Central Planning Board handled it in the interests of armament.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, do you want us to understand that the Central Planning Board only met every 3 months?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes, approximately. In very rare cases another meeting was called, especially if there were urgent problems to be solved. I remember, for instance, the case when it was said that agriculture was not getting enough nitrogen and that if the nitrogen quota were too small, agricultural production would suffer. In view of this State Secretary Backe asked for a meeting to be called and this took place at the Central Planning Board office.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Would you testify that Sauckel did not report to the Central Planning Board, at a meeting at which you were present, that out of all the labor that came to Germany only 200,000 came voluntarily -- out of the millions who came only 200,000 came voluntarily?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I cannot remember that.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Do you say that the Central Planning Board never discussed labor questions?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: At the Central Planning Board only demands for labor were submitted, and the quota holders to whom raw materials were allocated also demanded the necessary labor. Only very rough figures were given and then passed on to the Plenipotentiary General for the Allocation of Labor.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What about prisoners of war?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: With these the Central Planning Board was not at all concerned, as it was given only rough figures. For instance, if some branch of industry needed so many thousand workers, they were asked for.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What about concentration camp labor?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: The distribution of labor was dealt with by the labor exchanges. The Central Planning Board had nothing to do with it.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Are you familiar with a letter dated 9 March 1944, reciting that 36,000 concentration camp prisoners were now being used and wanting an increase to 90,000?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I do not know about these demands.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Do you know about the use of Russian prisoners of war in manning antiaircraft guns?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: After Goering closed the unauthorized concentration camps, did you know that the number of concentration camps increased very greatly in Germany?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: This I do not know. What happened after they were turned over to Himmler is beyond my knowledge. It may be that a large number of concentration camps was then set up.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: How do you come to know about Goering's relations with Himmler? Did he tell you?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Goering once spoke about it, and I concluded that the relations were not at all good.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Do you know about the appointment of Kaltenbrunner as head of the Austrian State Police after the Anschluss?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Do you know who obtained that appointnient for Kaltenbrunner?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: No, I have no idea.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, you say that Goering and you were in Munich on the night or nights of the anti-Jewish riots in Germany?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Was Goebbels also there?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Go ahead; do you want to say something else?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: On 9 November we traveled from Munich to Berlin, so Goebbels could not be there then.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Why could he not be there?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Because the Reich Marshal, with his entourage, traveled in his train to Berlin.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I mean, did you know that Goebbels was in Munich before these uprisings?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes, that I heard afterwards -- that Goebbels was in Munich. All National Socialist leaders were in Munich because 9 November was a day when all of them met.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And Goebbels spoke in Munich on the Jewish question that night did he not?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: That I do not know. I do not remember the speech.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Goering was there to attend the meeting of the National Socialist leaders, was he not?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes, on 9 November the entire leadership of the National Socialist Party met in Munich. It was an anniversary meeting.

turns.speaker: AIR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And Goering attended regularly?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Of course he did.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And you did?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I did also.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, Hess attended?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: As I said, all National Socialist leaders always attended if they possibly could. Nobody ever failed to attend unless he were ill, or prevented by official duties.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Which of the defendants in the dock attended those meetings? Ribbentrop, of course?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Ribbentrop, certainly.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Keitel ?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I assume so. MR, JUSTICE JACKSON: Kaltenbrunner?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I never saw Kaltenbrunner, because Kaltenbrunner held a public post only during the latter years, and during these years the meetings were not as regular as before.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Rosenberg , of course, was there?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Of course, as I said before.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And Frank and Frick ?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Certainly.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And Streicher ?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Not during the latter years, I do not think so; but previously he attended.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: When was that, during the latter years?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: As far as I know, Streicher did not attend during the latter years, but I do not know for certain.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: He attended in November 1938 when the anti-Jewish uprisings took place, did he not?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I believe so, because at that time Streicher was still in Nuremberg.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: He was very active, was he not?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I did not understand the question quite correctly.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: He was very active in the anti-Jewish matters, was he not?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes; this is generally known.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And did he see Funk at those meetings?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I believe that Funk frequently attended these meetings.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What was the subject considered at this meeting of 9 November, the night of the anti-Jewish uprising?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I do not know of any discussions as there was always a fixed program on that day, and I did not know about anything else, nor can the Reich Marshal have known.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Who was the adjutant who informed him on his arrival the next morning that something had happened during the night?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: This I cannot say exactly as the adjutants were always changing. I only know that an adjutant came and reported.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What did he say that happened?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: He reported that during the night anti-Jewish riots had taken place and were still going on; that shop windows had been smashed, goods thrown into the streets. Goering was infuriated about this.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What was he infuriated about?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: About the riots.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You mean that he was taking the part of the Jews?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: About the entire action.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You mean that he was taking the part of the Jews?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Goering always showed a different attitude to the Jewish question.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You just tell us what it was. You may go into all details. Tell us what his attitude was.

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: He always showed a moderate attitude towards the Jews.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Such as fining them a billion Reichsmark right after the fire, right after these outrages? You know that he did that, do you not?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes. The Fuehrer demanded it.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You know that the Fuehrer is dead, do you not? Do you know that for a fact?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes, I know he is dead.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: That is generally understood, is it not, among all of you, that the Fuehrer is dead?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: So the Fuehrer ordered the Reich Marshal to levy a fine of a billion Reichsmark? Who ordered the confiscation of the insurance of the Jews a few days after this assault?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: That I do not know. I can no longer remember the details.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Do you not remember that that was Goering's order?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I cannot recall it now.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Why did Goering go to Hitler to get this stopped? Why did he not go to the head of the police, which is supposed to prevent crime?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Naturally he went to the highest chief so that an authoritative order could be given for these riots to cease immediately. MR. JUSTICi JACKSON: Did he have any idea who had started them?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: It had gone round that Goebbels had instigated these riots.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did he know that the Gestapo and SS also participated?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I do not know. As far as I know the SS did not participate.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did the Gestapo?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: No, I do not know that either.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: So he went to Hitler to complain about Goebbels instigating these riots, is that the fact?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes, that is correct.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: So that he knew the next morning that these riots against the Jews had been instigated by members of the Government?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You were interrogated at Obersalzberg, the interrogation center, on the 4th of October of last year by Dr. Kempner of our staff, were you not?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And you stated in the beginning of your interrogation that you would not give any testimony against your former superior, Reich Marshal Goering, and that you regarded Goering as the last big man of the Renaissance; the last great example of a man from the Renaissance period; that he had given you the biggest job of your life and it would be unfaithful and disloyal to give any testimony against him; is that what you said?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes, that is more or less what I said.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And that is still your answer?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: No further questions.

turns.speaker: TEE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Do any other members of the Prosecution wish to examine this witness?

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: Perhaps you can remember, Witness, the conference of the heads of the German authorities in the occupied territories which took place on 6 August 1942 under the chairmanship of Defendant Goering.

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I cannot remember straight off what conference that could have been.

turns.speaker: GEN.RUDENKO
turns.text: Perhaps you can recall that after this conference of 6 August you circulated the minutes to all the ministers. The appendix to these minutes showed how much foodstuff and other raw materials should be supplied to Germany by the occupied territories?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I cannot remember offhand.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: I shall put before you a document signed by you yourself which gives proof of this meeting.

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes, I have read it.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: You remember that you circulated this document, do you not?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: The document shows that certain figures were fixed as to how much foodstuff should be sent to Germany: 1,200,000 tons from France, Belgium, the Netherlands, and Norway. From Russia, 3,000,000 tons of grain were to be sent to Germany, et cetera . Do you not consider such deliveries to be a spoliation of the occupied territories?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: It was a matter of course that the occupied territories had to make every effort in contributing to the food supply. Quotas were imposed on the occupied territories which they could meet or, if they were not in a position to do so, they could subsequently ask for modifications.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: You said something about "squeezing out," I think?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: No, I never talked of squeezing out. I said it was a matter of course that the occupied territories had to contribute to the food supply with all the means at their disposal.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: That the occupied territories had to contribute?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: Had these occupied territories asked Germany to come and rule over them?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I did not quite understand that question.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: I do not suppose you did. I now want to ask you another question in connection with this. You did not see that this was plunder, but do you not recall that Goering himself ...

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: No, this could not have been plunder.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: Goering himself at the same meeting said in his address that he intended to plunder the occupied territories systematically; you do not remember his expression "systematically plunder"?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: No, I do not know this expression.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: No, you do not remember. Perhaps you can recall that at the same meeting, when addressing the leaders of the occupied territories, he said to them, "You are sent there not to work for the welfare of the people you are in charge of, but you are sent there in order to squeeze out of that country everything possible." Do you remember these words of the Defendant Goering?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: No, I cannot remember these words.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: You cannot remember?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: And you do not recall a lengthy correspondence between Goering and Rosenberg in which Rosenberg insisted that all functions relative to the economic exploitation of the occupied territories of the Soviet Union should be taken away from the military economic offices and transferred to the ministry headed by Rosenberg?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: No, I do not recall this letter.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: You do not know. And in connection with this you do not remember that this correspondence did not lead to a final settlement of the question?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I do not know anything about this correspondence.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: You do not know anything, do you? In 1944 do you not recall that ...

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: I should like to point out that the interpretation is very incomplete and hard to understand. We ourselves do not fully understand the questions either.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: I suggest it is not my fault if the witness does not get all my questions. [Turning to the witness.] Do you not recall that in 1944, after the Red Army had driven the German troops from the Ukraine, Goering, wishing to shelve the question of the economic exploitation of the Ukraine, wrote to Rosenberg that it should be postponed until a more opportune time, and Goering mentioned a second seizure of the Ukraine and other Soviet territories?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Is this supposed to have happened in 1944?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: No, I cannot remember it.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: I shall not argue about it. [Turning to the President.] Evidently, Mr. President, you wish to adjourn now. I have a few more questions, but I assume it will be convenient to resume after the adjournment.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Yes. [The Tribunal recessed until 1400 hours.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The Tribunal will adjourn at 4:30 today.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: Witness, I intend to hand you a document which is a letter addressed to you by the Permanent Delegate of the Reich Minister for the Occupied Eastern Territories. This is Document Number USSR-174. I want you to read it and say whether you have ever seen this letter before. You will see that this document begins with the words, "Honorable Secretary of State and dear Party Comrade Korner." This letter deals with the unification of economic leadership.

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I have taken note of this document. I definitely received it.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: You have received it; that is quite obvious. As is quite clear from this communication, the question is that of holding a special meeting under your leadership.

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: Therefore I may conclude that you were a very close collaborator of the Defendant Goering in the matter of the so-called unification of economic leadership?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes, for the conference mentioned.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: One last question. Do you confirm that the Defendant Goering as Delegate for the Four Year Plan, was at the head of both the civilian and the military German organizations dealing with the economic exploitation of all the occupied territories, and that you were his closest collaborator where these economic measures were concerned?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: The conference mentioned in this document never took place. The unification of economic leadership was a problem which arose, but which never really became a fact. Therefore the conference mentioned was superfluous.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: The problem was not solved, because of circumstances over which you had no control. It depended on the advance of the Red and Allied Armies. Am I right?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I did not understand the question clearly enough to answer it.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: You say that the question was not solved. I ask you, is it not a fact thatthe problem was not solved because of circumstances which did not depend on yourselves? You were prevented by the Red and Allied Armies?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I believe that at the time this letter was sent no such influence could have been felt. The question of the comprehensive organization of economic matters in occupied territories did not, as a fact, materialize because it was opposed by other circumstances.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: I do not mean to discuss these causes with you at the present moment, but you have not yet answered my last question. I asked: Do you confirm that Goering, as Delegate for the Four Year Plan, was at the head of both the civilian and the military German organizations dealing with the economic exploitation of all the occupied territories, and that you were his closest collaborator?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: As far as the exploitation of occupied countries is concerned, it cannot be dealt with in this manner. The Four Year Plan had the possibility of exerting influence in economic questions in the occupied countries, but it was done only if it was absolutely necessary. In general it did not concern itself with such problems. The authorities who took care of economic matters in the occupied countries were the military commanders or the heads of the civil administration. In the East was the Economic Staff East and Rosenberg's Ministry. Only if there was a matter between the military and the economic authorities or between German departments, 0here there was a dispute or a disagreement, could the Four Year Plan be drawn in. The Reich Marshal in those cases could make special decisions, but that was in very, very few cases as, for instance, in the case of this conference mentioned today, concerning the occupied countries having to help supply foodstuffs for Europe. We had the right, since in the occupied territories not only in the East but also in the West, we carried out many new developments in the sphere of agriculture. In the West I can point out.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: What right are you discussing?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I speak of the right which Germany had to share in the agricultural production of these countries, because we introduced many new developments there. I would like to point out that in the East, the regions which had been completely devastated, which had no seed, no machines, and with greatest difficulty...

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: Who gave that right to the Germans?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: The right? It is only natural that once we have occupied a country and built it up, we are entitled to share in the surplus. We had to take care of the whole of Europe and we knew what anxieties and problems we encountered in the occupied countries.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: I asked you, where did the Germans get the right?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I am no jurist. Therefore I cannot answer the question.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: But you were talking about German rights.

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I am speaking only of the natural right that if we built up a country we should share in the results of that work of development.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: After you had devastated these areas?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Germany did not devastate these areas, especially not in any agricultural respect. We, in fact, instituted great developments. I remember, in the West, that some parts of France were completely devastated and our organizations performed reconstrtiction work there. Thus we rebuilt the uncultivated land which we found in France, through a German organization which had reconstructed whole areas in Reich territory, and repatriated French people there, giving them the possibility of working again as peasants and sharing in the agricultural production of the country. In the East we found territories whose agriculture had been greatly damaged through the war. All the machines had disappeared. All the tractors had been taken away by the Russians, and all agricultural implements had been taken away or destroyed. There we had to start in the most elementary and primitive way to build up agriculture again. That it was possible for us in the years of our occupation in the Eastito restore agriculture, German initiative and German machinery alone are to be thanked.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: Did German, initiative also include, together with the restoration of agricultural measures and developments, a vast net of concentration camps which you established in the occupied countries? Was that also included in the extent of the German initiative?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I had nothing to do with that problem, and can say nothing about it.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: But I am asking you this question ...

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: And therefore I do not understand what you mean.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: You are not sufficiently informed on the question of concentration camps, but it would appear that you are quite well informed, or appear to be informed, on restoration work in the agricultural field?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Natilrally, I know a great deal about the rehabilitation of agricultural areas.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: But you know nothing about concentration camps?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I was not concerned with these matters.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: You knew nothing about the fact that millions were being annihilated by the German occupational authorities?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: No, I knew nothing about it.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: You really knew nothing about it?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: I have only just found out about it.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: Only now?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: I have no further question to ask.

turns.speaker: HERR GEORG BOEHM (Counsel for SA)
turns.text: Witness, do you know that Heines was Chief of Police at Breslau?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I asked defendants' counsel at the end of the examination by Dr. Stahmer whether they wished to ask any questions, and they said they did not wish to ask any questions. Therefore it is not your turn now to ask any questions.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: Mr. President. In the interrogation by Mr. Justice Jackson a point arose which I did not know of before and which calls for comment. It concerns the Chief of Police, Heines. May I be allowed to put two or three questions to the witness so that the point in question may be clarified?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Very well. We hope you will not take too long.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: I will try to be brief, Mr. President. Thank you. [Turning to the witness.] Witness, do you know that Heines was Chief of Police at Breslau?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: Further, do you know that in that capacity he was in charge of the prisons in Breslau?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Of course, the Police Chief is in charge of prisons.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: Do you know whether at the time in question when this camp was set up, the police prisons of Breslau were overcrowded?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: That I do not know. I mentioned the case of Heines only as one of the camps which at that time were set up without the permission of the Prime Minister or the Minister of the Interior.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: Then you also know that Heines could establish this camp merely in his capacity as Chief of Police?

turns.speaker: KORNER
turns.text: Yes, that may be.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Stahmer, have you any questions to ask?

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: I have no further questions to put to the witness.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Then the witness may retire.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHNER
turns.text: With the permission of the Tribunal I call as next witness, Field Marshal Kesselring. [The witness Kesselring took the stand.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you tell me your name?

turns.speaker: ALBERT KESSELRING (Witness)
turns.text: Albert Kesselring.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you repeat this oath after me: I swear by God -- the Almighty and Omniscient -- that I will speak the pure truth -- and will withhold and add nothing. [The witness repeated the oath in German.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: You may sit if you wish.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Witness, since when have you served with the Luftwaffe?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: Since 1 October 1933.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: What rank did you hold on your transfer to the Luftwaffe?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: Up to that time I was a colonel and officer commanding artillery in Dresden. Then I was retired as air commodore.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: You helped to build up the Luftwaffe?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: During the first 3 years I was Chief of the Administrative Office, subsequently Chief of the General Staff, and I then served in the Gruppenkommando.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Was the Luftwaffe being built up for defensive or aggressive purposes?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: The German Luftwaffe was purely a weapon of defense. I must, however, add the comment that the single plane as well as the whole of an air force by its very nature is an aggressive weapon. Even in land fighting, mere defense unaccompanied by offensive movements is considered not to lead to any appreciable results or successes. This applies to a still greater degree to air warfare. The air force covers a wider range, both for defense and attack. This had been realized by the Reich Marshal and his generals. It is obvious that when an air force is being built up, only light machines are produced, or are the first types to reach the units. Thus, up to 1936-37 we had only light craft, fighters, Stukas, reconnaissance planes, and a few "old sledges" as we called them, such as Ju 52, Do 11 and D 13 -- all obsolete bomber types. One may hold the view that defense can be successfully conducted with these light craft. On the other hand, I should like to point to the end of the World War, when the German defensive air force was smashed by the offensive air force of the enemy.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Stahmer, the Tribunal thinks the witness is dealing with this matter in far too great detail.

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: I will go on. Up to 1937-38 there was no offensive air force, especially no bombers, and the bombers which were built later had neither the range nor the load capacity necessary for an offensive weapon. There were no four-engine bombers.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHNER
turns.text: Did you play any part in the attack on Warsaw?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: As Chief of Air Fleet 1, I led this attack.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did the military situation at the time justify this attack, and how was it carried out?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: Several attacks were made on Warsaw. In the German view, Warsaw was a fortress, and, moreover, it had strong air defenses. Thus the stipulations of the Hague Convention for land warfare, which can analogously be applied to air warfare, were fulfilled. As to the first phase of the attack on Warsaw, according to the operational principle governing the employment of the Luftwaffe, the enemy air force and the aircraft factories in the immediate vicinity of the airfields were to be attacked. These attacks were in my opinion fully justified and they comply with the rules. The second phase concerns the combating of the operational movements of the Poles. I may add that Warsaw is a junction for northern and central Poland. When our long-range reconnaissance reported -- this was confirmed by the final phase -- that the railway stations were crammed with material and that reinforcements in increasing numbers were moving on Warsaw, the air attack on these movements was ordered and carried out. It was mainly directed against railway stations and sidings and the Vistula bridges. For the execution of these attacks I detailed Stukas and ground "strafer" aircraft, because the precision of these machines afforded the guarantee that mainly the military targets would be hit. The third phase was the shelling of Warsaw. I consider the shelling to be an army action in which, at the request of the army, small units of the Luftwaffe were employed against military targets. I myself was over Warsaw, and after practically every air attack I consulted the army commanders about the execution. From my own experiences and reports I can assert that everything that was humanly possible was done to hit military targets only and to spare civilian targets.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Can you confirm conclusively that these attacks were kept throughout within the limits of military necessity?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: Absolutely.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHNER
turns.text: Did you play any part in the attack on Rotterdam?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: As Air Force Chief 2, to which rank I had been promoted, I led air attacks on Holland, Belgium, and France, and the airborne corps operated under my command also. The airborne corps was commanded by General Student, who asked for his paratroops to be supported by a bomber attack. General Student had such a comprehensive knowledge of the ground situation that he alone must be considered responsible for preparation and execution of the attack. The Fourth Air Corps was ordered to provide air support, and one group, the smallest unit necessary for this purpose, was employed. The attack was carried out solely in accordance with the tactical requirements and technical possibilities. The orders of General Student reached my command very early. Thus all preparations could be made leisurely according to plan. At the instance of the Reich Marshal the unit was informed of possible changes within Rotterdam and of the approach of Panzer divisions. The objective set by General Student was quite elear as to extent, central and key points, and occupation. It was not difficult for seasoned troops to grasp the objective. There was radio communication between General Student's command, my staff, and other staffs, including the Commander-in-Chief of the Luftwaffe. Any interruption of this communication could only have been a very short one as radio orders were transmitted by me or the Reich Marshal. The technique at that time made it possible to maintain contact through this radio communication between the tactical ground station and the flying unit via its ground station. The ground communications usual at that time such as flags, flares, and signal code designations at the front were maintained according to plan. They functioned without a hitch. In accordance with its training and its orders the formation had sent out a reconnaissance aircraft which kept them informed of the situation and the objective. In addition, by order of the Reich Marshal, there followed a General Staff officer attached to my air fleet who had the same mission.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Had the order been given that the situation and the objective should be...

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: I myself never had any doubt that the attack had to be carried out; I was only not quite sure whether or not it should be repeated. And this was the question to which the signals referred. Judging from my knowledge of General Student and -- I stress this particularly -- his technique in leading an attack and his clearly stated requirements, I had to expect the attack to be carried out. The attack was carried out according to plan and time schedule. The report that the target had been accurately bombed came through very quickly together with the message that no further attacks were necessary. During the 3 days of fighting in Holland the Commander-in-Chief of the Luftwaffe was kept well informed. Particularly on the third day, that is, the day I am talking of, the Reich Marshal in his outspoken manner intervened more than usual in the direction of the air fleet and did, in my opinion, everything that could possibly be done from such a high position. I do not remember any message to the effect that the bomber attack was no longer warranted by the tactical situation.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Bombs are said to have been dropped when negotiations about capitulation had already started.

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: As I said, no message to this effect had been received by the command, neither had the formation operating over Rotterdam picked up a message from the ground. Probably some confusion occurred at the command In Rotterdam itself of which I know nothing. Neither do I know about the agreements reached between General Student and the officer commanding the Dutch troops in Rotterdam. I wanted later to have a talk with General Student on this question, but it was not possible because of his having received a serious head injury. If, contrary to my firm conviction, the attack had been no longer warranted by the situation, this would be most regrettable. As a soldier of 42 years' standing, as an artillery man, as an airman, as a General Staff officer, and as a leader for many years, I wish to make it clear that this case was one of those unforeseeable coincidences of war which, I am sorry to say, occur in the armed services of all countries more frequently than one might think; only the world does not know.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: How do you explain the big fires that still broke out in Rotterdam?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: When I received the report from the formation I was very pleasantly surprised to learn that the effect of the bombing was confined to the target area, but this war has shown that most of the destruction is not caused bythe bombs themselves, but by the spreading of fires. Unfortunately a bomb had hit a margarine or some other factory in Rotterdam, causing oil to run out and the fire to spread. As after the attack the capitulation was already effective, it should have been possible to prevent the fires from spreading by bringing in the fire services and the troops.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: What were the military consequences of this attack?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: The immediate consequence of the attack was the surrender of the Rotterdam troops. General Wenninger, who was air attache at the time and who later was attached to my air fleet, told me that in consequence of this attack the whole of the Dutch Army capitulated.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did you lead the attack on Coventry in November 1940?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: As Chief of Air Fleet 2 I took part in this attack, without any doubt. I cannot say now whether Air Fleet 3 took part in it as well, but I did.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: What was the object of the attack?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: According to the target index kept by the archives department of the Commander-in-Chief of the Luftwaffe, Coventry was an English armament center; it was known as "Little Essen." This index was compiled with meticulous care by experts, engineers, and officers, and contained maps, charts, photographs, description of targets, key points, et cetera . I myself, as well as my men, was fully familiar with these details. Furthermore, I had the aforementioned General Wenninger and several engineers with the Commander-in-Chief of the Luftwaffe give lectures to the troops about targets, in order to make them acquainted with the nature of the targets, their vulnerability, and the effects of an air attack. Preparations for an attack were made most conscientiously. I was very often present and the Reich Marshal himself occasionally inspected them. The case of Coventry was extremely simple, as during those nights favorable weather conditions prevailed, so that Coventry could be reached without radio navigation. The distribution of the targets in Coventry was likewise very simple, so that bombs could be dropped without the help of flares, and it was hardly possible to miss the target. But bombs follow the same law as other projectiles; in other words, in land and air warfare dispersion covers a wide range. With an air force this is the further peculiarity that if strong formations are employed not the individual target but only the target area as a whole can be aimed at, which naturally causes a deviation from the target itself. By order of the Commander-inChief of the Luftwaffe and on the reconnaissance pilot's own initiative, all hits and attacks were checked the following day by air photographs. The ground visibility was good but, as I already said in the case of Rotterdam, the destruction of the objective was not caused so much by the bombs themselves as by the spreading of fire. I do not know whether I should add anything further. The Hague Convention on land warfare did not provide for the requirements of air warfare. In order to avoid an arbitrary selection of targets, the Supreme Command had to go into the question and issue general directives based on the preamble to the Hague Convention, the literature published in the meantime, and finally, the special conditions governing the Luftwaffe itself. Only those targets which we considered admissible according to international law were assigned to the air fleet or formation. This did not exclude the reconsideration and change of targets in individual cases, which were discussed with the Commander-in-Chief of the Luftwaffe, and we took the responsibility ...

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: You are speaking too fast.

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: By personal visits and other means we impressed upon our units the need to study preparation, the dropping of bombs, aiming, the meteorological conditions, so carefully that the highest degree of accuracy could be obtained and regrettable deviations into the perimeter of the objectives could be avoided. The case of Coventry was particularly fortunate as it presented an important military target, and no one could speak of it as an attack directed against the civilian population.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: I have no more questions.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Does any other defense counsel wish to ask questions?

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Witness, since when were you commander of an army group?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: I became commander of an army group in September 1943 after, as commander of the German troops in the Supreme Command, I had already served in a supervisory capacity as far as general strategic and tactical questions were concerned.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: The army group which you led was in Italy?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: The army group was in the Mediterranean area.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Do you know the composition of the General Staff and High Command group as presented by the Prosecution?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: First I have a preliminary question. What is, strictly speaking, understood by the German General Staff of the individual branches of the Wehrmacht?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: The General Staff of the individual branches of the Wehrmacht comprises all those officers who assist the commanders-in-chief of the services and share their responsibility.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Would you please state how this group was composed and organized in the Luftwaffe, for instance?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: The General Staff of the Luftwaffe was the equivalent of the General Staff of the Army and these organizations were as alike as two pins. The General Staff consisted of the central department, called the Operations Staff in the Luftwaffe, headed by the Chief of the General Staff, the operational departments, the organizational groups, the departmental chiefs of the Luftwaffe, the supply office, et cetera . The various commands, from the air fleet down to the division, the ground staff and the Luftgaue, had General Staff officers attached to them to assist in the command. A chief of general staff no longer bore co-responsibility, as was previously customary, since this was held to be inconsistent with the Leadership Principle. These chiefs of general staffs and the chief of the central department of the General Staff exercisedtheir influence regarding military and ideological training on all the General Staff officers within the Wehrmacht, without prejudice to the responsibility of the individual military commander.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: If I summarize your reply that by General Staff of the Luftwaffe is meant the Chief of General Staff and the regimental staff officers, would I then be describing correctly the composition of the General Staff of the Luftwaffe?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: Most certainly.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Do you consider the term "General Staff" as has been employed in these proceedings to be in accordance with military usage?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: As I said before, the General Staff was composed of officers assisting in the command, which did not include the commanders and commanders-in-chief. According to German views they did not belong to that category, because not all the commanders and commanders-in-chief had had the same education and training as the General Staff officers. The cornmanders-in-chief were single individuals. They would be treated collectively only in connection with their rank as generals and for budget and pay purposes.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Would you consider it to be erroneous to apply the term "General Staff" to the high military conuxianders?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: According to the German conception it would be a misnomer.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Have at any time in the history of the Wehrmacht the high military commanders been subsumed under this group as is being done here?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: In Germany such subsumption was not indicated and for various reasons was not even admissible. Neither did the commanders-in-chief form a collective body to act in any way as a war council or as a similar assembly with definite tasks. They were not even, individually or collectively, members of the Reich Defense Council, but were only appointed ad hoc commanders of a front or a command post. To set up the commanders-in-chief as a collective body for any specific purpose was in my opinion quite impossible, for the simple reason that they were under the commander-in-chief of the Army, the Luftwaffe, or the Navy or under the High Command of the Armed Forces. Moreover, some were 100 percent under the German Supreme Command; others were 100 percent under Axis command. Some of them were under two different commands, some were independent commanders-in-chief, others were army commanders-in-chief subordinate to an army group.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: You are speaking too fast. Had the commanders-in-chief only to work out military problems set before them, or did they themselves draw up plans and submit them to Hitler for consideration?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: The commanders-in-chief were purely military leaders, responsible only for the task allotted to them. Within the scope of this task they could submit suggestions or improvements, et cetera , to the OKW or to the OKH, but their activities in the sense of collaboration were limited to these suggestions.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: You just mentioned improvements and modifications. Did this mean that the commanders-in-chief were expected to suggest modifications of a plan only from the military-technical aspect, or also to submit suggestions as to whether or not a plan should be carried out at all?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: Generally it meant suggestions for modifications from the military-technical aspect only. In matters of minor importance they had a say also as to policy. If, however, the highest authority had made a decision, the others kept silent.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: We will revert to this later. Did the "General Staff" group as presented here ever meet collectively?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Were there any rules providing for the organization of this group?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Did any members of this group ever suggest a departure from the rules of international law?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: I do not think so; rather the contrary.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Was there a frequent reshuffle of the holders of the offices which make up this group, or did they hold the offices for a long period?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: In the course of the later years the commandersin-chief and commanders were rather frequently reshuffled.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: What do you know about the conferences Hitler held with high-ranking military leaders?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: There were two kinds of conferences. First, an important address before a campaign to the higher leaders taking part in it. The object of the address was generally to inform the leaders of the situation and to brief them. In view of the Fuehrer's persuasive rhetoric it was hardly possible for us to take any stand in the matter, particularly as we were not informed about all the details. At such conferences discussions did not take place; they were not allowed. There sometimes followed military-tactical consultations, and every leader had the chance of putting forward and stressing his views and requests. As I have said, we had no say in political questions. We were, as is known, faced with the accomplished fact, which we as soldiers had to accept.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Did you attend a conference held by Hitler on 22 August 1939, that is, shortly before the Polish campaign started?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Was it not made known at the end of this conference that we had concluded a treaty with the Soviet Union?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: At the end, after the address, we were all called together again and informed that the message had just been received that Russia would adopt benevolent neutrality.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: What impression did this message have on you and the other high military leaders?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: It was a tremendous relief to me and to the others. Otherwise we could not have dismissed the possibility of an extension of the war toward the East. Now that Russia was going to hold herself aloof, the Luftwaffe at least -- I speak as an army commander -- had a superiority which guaranteed a rapid and decisive success, and which over and above this, in my opinion, would possibly prevent the expansion of the war.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: In any case, the message was a great relief to you?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: Yes, very great.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Witness, can you tell me whether members of the General Staff and OKW group ever met and had discussions with leading politicians and Party men?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: If I may speak for myself, I was operating both in the Mediterranean area and in the West. In the Mediterranean area I had to work with the Gauleiter Rainer and Hofer and then in the West with ...

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: That was not the point of the question. I wanted to know whether the high military leaders ever met and discussed any political plans with leading politicians.

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: No, no. That I can definitely say was not the case. We as soldiers generally did not bother about politics. Political decisions were made by the politicians and we had to carry them out.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Among military leaders, as a result of their many years of experience in the Wehrmacht, which foster the principle of giving the soldier a nonpolitical education, this attitude is customary, is it not?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: This policy has been developed in the German Army since the 18th century.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Do you know whether the higher military leaders had any contact with the Fifth Column?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: The military leadership had nothing to do with the Fifth Column. This was beneath us.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: What was your impression of the conference Hitler held with the higher military leaders before the Eastern campaign started? Was the situation presented to you in such a way that war had to be considered unavoidable?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: I had the definite impression that the purpose of the address to the leaders was to convince them of the necessity of the war as a preventive war; and that it was imperative to strike before the building up and the mobilization of the Russian armed forces became a danger to Germany.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Could you state the reasons for your impression?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: As I have already said, the purpose of the address was to give us a convincing picture of the general situation; of the military situation and its time schedule -- and it did convince us. In connection with the Russian campaign I should like to say that up to the last day of August I had no doubt...

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Witness, will you go more slowly please and have some consideration for the interpreters.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Would you please repeat the last answer.

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: I had still less reason to doubt Hitler's words because, up to the last moment, I, as Commander-in-Chief of Air Fleet 2, was engaged in operations against England and had had neither time nor the means to form a well-founded judgment of my own on the Russian situation. I had to confme myself...

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: This Trial has shown that the commandersin-chief are being made responsible for what is bound to happen in a war. I should like you to describe the daily routine of a commander-in-chief of an army group, an army, or an air fleet.

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: The daily routine depended of course on the personality of the individual leader. If I may speak of myself...

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Witness, I ask you to be very brief.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Witness -- Dr. Laternser, surely, that is cumulative to what the witness has already been saying, and likely to be very long. About the description of the day of a commander, this witness already said the commander had nothing to do with politics and nothing to do with the staff. Why should we be troubled with what the commander's day consists of?

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Mr. President, I attach particular importance to this question for the following reasons: In view of the range of a commander-in-chief's activities, especially at the front, not every report can reach him because even reports from his own sector have to be dealt with by the respective officers. Thus, only those reports come to him which are of particular importance and of a decisive nature and which have a direct bearing on the conduct of the action.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Give it in that way then, rather than giving the witness a full day to describe.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Very well, I shall put it that way. Witness, in view of the range of your activities as commanderin-chief did every report reach you, or only those which, after having been studied by the respective officers, were found to be of such importance that they had to be submitted to the commander-in-chief?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: Especially when an action was in Progress all reports could not reach the commander-in-chief. In my particular case this was still less possible as I spent 50 to 70 percent of my time at the front. The staffs of the armies, air fleets, and navy units had to retain a responsibility of their own within their competence.

turns.speaker: DR.ILATERNSER
turns.text: Did the many activities of a commander-in-chief allow all reports on violations of international law, even of a minor nature, to be submitted to him?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: This had to be aimed at. I doubt, however, for the afore-mentioned reasons, whether this was possible in every case.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: In this matter, therefore, the commander-in-chief had to rely on his staff, had he not?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: Yes; 100 percent.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Were you commander-in-chief of an air fleet on the Eastern front from June to November 1941?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Did you hear anything about the extermination of Jews in the East?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Did you hear anything about the Einsatzgruppen of the SS?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: Nothing. I did not even know the name of these units.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Did you get to know anything about the regrettable order that Russian commissars were to be shot after their capture?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: I heard of this order at the end of the war. The air fleet, not being engaged in ground fighting, had actually nothing to do with this question. I think I can safely say the Luftwaffe knew nothing whatsoever about it. Though I very frequently had personal dealings with Field Marshal Von Bock, with commanders of armies and armored units, none of these gentlemen ever told me of such an order.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Did you know about the Commando Order?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: Yes, I did.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: And what did you think of this order?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: I considered such an order, received by me as commander-in-chief in the Mediterranean, where I held a double post, as not binding for me, but as the outline of an order which left me a free hand in its application. On this question I held the view that it was for me, as commander-in-chief, to decide whether a Commando action was contrary to international law'or whether it was tactically justified. The view adopted more and more by the army group, which view was directed by me, was that personnel in uniform who had been sent out on a definite tactical task were to be treated and considered as soldiers in accordance with the provisions of the Hague Convention for land warfare.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: The Commando Order was consequently not applied within your command?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: In one case, yes, it was certainly applied.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Which case do you mean?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: I mean the case of General Dostler.

turns.speaker: DR. I.ATERNSER
turns.text: The case of General Dostler has already been mentioned in this Trial. Did you know about this case when it was pending?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: As a witness under oath I have stated that I cannot remember this case. I think there are two reasons why I was not informed of it. Firstly, after a conversation with my chief, who spoke to another commander about it, it appeared that none of us knew anything. Secondly, because of the gigantic operations on the Southern Front, I was more often absent than not from my headquarters.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Witness, if you had been called upon to make a decision on the Dostler case, how would you have decided?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: I am not well enough acquainted with the case. I know it only from hearsay.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I do not think we can try Dostler's case, or that this witness should give his conclusions, inasmuch as Dostler's case has been tried by a competent court and that issue is disposed of. I have no objection to any facts that inform this Tribunal, but his conclusion as to the guilt of his fellow officer is hardly helpful.

turns.speaker: TBE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Particularly as he said he cannot remember.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: I withdraw the question. Witness, can you quote other cases where the Commando Order was not applied in your area?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: Small scale landings behind the lines at Commazzio, south of Venice, also airborne landings north of Albenda in the region of Genoa and minor actions in the Lago di Ortona district. I am convinced the troops adopted this general view and acted accordingly.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: You were commander-in-chief of an air fleet in the East. What can you say about the treatment of the Russian civilian population during the campaign?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
turns.text: I was in Russia until the end of November and I can say only that the population and the troops were on the best of terms, and that the field kitchens were used everywhere for the benefit of the poor and the children; also that the morality of the Russian woman, which, as is known, is on a high level, was respected by the German soldiers to a remarkable extent. I know that my doctors, during the hours of attendance, were frequently consulted by the Russian population. I remember this, because the doctors spoke to me about the fortitude they showed in enduring pain. The war passed so quickly over the plains as far as Smolensk that the whole area presented quite a peaceful aspect; peasants were at work, fairly large herds of cattle were grazing, and when I visited the area I found the small dwellings intact.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Did you hear of any excesses committed by German soldiers in the East? Whenever cases of violations of international law were reported to you, did you take action with all the means at your disposal?

turns.speaker: KESSELRING
